## [03:00 - 06:01]

there's different things going on. So one of the things about this, and as a note for you, is when I look at this data here, so it's costing me $48 to get someone to start the checkout process, but how does the price drop on the cost per purchase? This is something which in Facebook, you get late attributions, you get view through attributions. And also one of the things that happens quite often when you have multiple payment options, as that happens on this page, people can bypass your checkout, for example, if they use PayPal. So for example, on this store, on the cart page, you can go straight to PayPal and bypass the actual, for this store, the Shopify payment page. So that means that there are people going through the PayPal journey and firing that cost per purchase pixel. So therefore, this isn't quite a valid number when I look at this. Now, what I could do is duplicate this audience, leave this one running, so I create a duplicate, and simply take out 25 to 34 and let them run side by side. One of the good things about CBO, and why I'm a big fan of CBO, is because when you've got multiple audiences in one campaign running, CBO takes care of things like auction overlap. And it makes sure that you don't get the general instability that you would have using AdSET budgets. So one option is to do that. ## [06:01 - 09:02]

Maybe that's actually worth removing. Maybe that's where a lot of my spend is being wasted. Or maybe there's something I'm not seeing where there's a better advantage. So this is how I go through my drill down reports to go deeper and deeper into finding out where my opportunities are. So, for example, this is running in the US. Maybe there's a regional aspect to this as well, that certain regions are getting higher spend. I don't know. And that's part of the investigation, part of the insights work. Although these are smaller spends, I'm looking at Georgia there, for example. So Georgia spent $34. Actually, the cost per content view is higher than many other states, and it's not converting. Maybe there's something in Georgia that is affecting people's conversion rate. I do not know. But the question here is, what is the next action that I should take? Now, taking those breakdowns away, I would personally leave that for the time being, because I don't want to rock the boat. I want to see how far I can push this performance. And also, bear in mind, when it comes to CBO, when you've got multiple ad sets like this running in your campaign, Facebook is trying to optimize for your overall ROAS. So your ROAS at the ad set level may fluctuate up and down. So this one might be 1.72 today. Might have been better the day before and worse the day after, for example. But Facebook's managing that budget day to day. So actually, by impacting that ad set's learning by taking out an age range, could actually be detrimental. So that's just something to bear in mind. And then actually, the next level down is, well, let's look at the ads. Like, let's look at the ads and see, maybe there's a bigger opportunity to optimize. And you can see we've already done that. So we've trimmed two of the ads that were underperforming and left the two that were performing better to run. Now, again, with CBO, it's worth giving Facebook the time to test and optimize what you've done. And we can see a massive difference in the cost per initiative checkout and ultimately the conversion rate and the ROAS. So there's something about these two ads here that didn't resonate and didn't convert, and these two did. That gives us ideas for what to do next. Now, if, for example, this ad here stops performing and we have to pause this, and we're only left with one ad, then we've got a different set of decisions to make. Do we fire up new ads from our testing and add them? Or actually, what I would do is, especially if I don't have any ads, if I've got one ad left, I just duplicate it, maybe once or twice and let it run. Because that's the way that Facebook works in its auction, is it may drop an ad to someone it thinks he's gonna buy, but that ad would have been better for someone else. And actually, if you're running duplicates of the same ad, you might have a higher chance of getting that user that's gonna click and convert. So what I want you to take away from this is, the analysis part goes as deep as you're willing to go, but understanding where your biggest influences are

## [12:02 - 12:23]

that are actually working better with certain age ranges. This comes through the analysis. So it's really important that you go through and you find your opportunities, whatever your main metric is, whether it's ROAS or CPA, the same thing applies where you can break down your performance and you can see where the opportunities are before you get to the stage of scaling.
